title: Die Geschichte vom Daumenlutscher
source: Heinrich Hoffmann, Der Struwwelpeter, Rütten & Loening, Frankfurt am Main, 564. Auflage — the canonical later text in modernized spelling (via Project Gutenberg #24571). Fresh Lectorium translation from the German.
about: Der Struwwelpeter (first published 1845) is Heinrich Hoffmann's famous picture book of cautionary verses — darkly comic warnings in which disobedient children meet spectacular ends. Here Konrad ignores his mother's warning about thumb-sucking, and the tailor with his great scissors comes. The gleeful exaggeration is the point: the poems mock the moralizing tale even while telling one.

sentences.t:
»Konrad,« sprach die Frau Mama,
»ich geh aus und du bleibst da.
sentences.l:
“Konrad,” spoke the Lady Mama,
“I go out and you stay here.
sentences.i:
“Konrad,” said his mother,
“I’m going out, and you’re staying here.
sentences.n: »Frau Mama« is the polite 19th-century nursery style — children spoke of their mother as »Frau Mama«, roughly ‘my lady mother.’ »sprach« is the old storybook past of »sprechen« (like English ‘spake’). »ich geh« drops the -e of »gehe« for the meter — verse does this constantly (»komm« below, too). And »da« here means ‘here (at home),’ not ‘there.’

sentences.t:
Sei hübsch ordentlich und fromm,
bis nach Haus ich wieder komm.
sentences.l:
Be nicely orderly and good,
until to home I again come.
sentences.i:
Be nice and neat and well-behaved
until I come back home.
sentences.n: »hübsch« before another adjective is adverbial — ‘nicely, properly,’ not ‘pretty.’ »fromm« in older German means ‘good, well-behaved’ (today it means ‘pious’) — the same word Cinderella’s mother uses. The word order »bis nach Haus ich wieder komm« is bent for the rhyme; plain prose would be »bis ich wieder nach Haus komme«.

sentences.t:
Und vor allem, Konrad, hör!
lutsche nicht am Daumen mehr;
sentences.l:
And before all, Konrad, hear!
suck not on-the thumb more;
sentences.i:
And above all, Konrad, listen:
no more sucking your thumb;
sentences.n: »vor allem« = ‘above all’ (literally ‘before all’). »hör!« and »lutsche!« are bare du-imperatives. German says »am Daumen lutschen« — ‘to suck on the thumb’ — with the definite article where English says ‘your’: with body parts, the owner is taken as obvious. »nicht … mehr« = ‘no longer, not any more.’

sentences.t:
denn der Schneider mit der Scher
kommt sonst ganz geschwind daher,
und die Daumen schneidet er
ab, als ob Papier es wär.«
sentences.l:
for the tailor with the shears
comes otherwise quite swiftly along,
and the thumbs cuts he
off, as if paper it were.”
sentences.i:
or else the tailor with his scissors
will come rushing in,
and he’ll cut your thumbs clean off,
as if they were paper.”
sentences.n: »abschneiden« (‘to cut off’) is a separable verb, and Hoffmann plays a famous trick with it: the »ab-« is snipped off its own verb and lands at the start of the next line — the line break cuts, just like the scissors. »sonst« = ‘otherwise, or else.’ »die Scher« is »die Schere« clipped for the rhyme. »als ob … wär« (= »wäre«) takes the subjunctive: ‘as if it were paper.’ »geschwind« = ‘swiftly’ — old-fashioned now; today »schnell«.

sentences.t:
Fort geht nun die Mutter und
wupp! den Daumen in den Mund.
sentences.l:
Away goes now the mother and
whoop! the thumb into the mouth.
sentences.i:
Now off goes the mother, and —
pop! in goes the thumb, into his mouth.
sentences.n: The second line has no verb at all: the sound-word »wupp!« plus the accusative of direction (»den Daumen in den Mund«) does all the work — the thumb is in before any verb could arrive. Even the »und« dangles at the line’s end, mid-leap. Fronting »Fort geht …« puts the leaving first, with the verb second as German requires.

sentences.t:
Bauz! da geht die Türe auf,
und herein in schnellem Lauf
springt der Schneider in die Stub
zu dem Daumen-Lutscher-Bub.
sentences.l:
Bang! there goes the door open,
and in, in swift run,
leaps the tailor into the parlor
to the thumb-sucker-boy.
sentences.i:
Bang! the door flies open,
and in at a run
leaps the tailor, into the room,
straight for the little thumb-sucking boy.
sentences.n: »aufgehen« (‘to open’) is separable: »geht … auf«. »die Türe« is an older by-form of »die Tür«. »Stub« and »Bub« are »Stube« (parlor) and »Bube« (boy) with the final -e clipped for the rhyme — »Bub« is still the everyday word for ‘boy’ in southern Germany. »Bauz!« is the crash of the door; the compound »Daumen-Lutscher-Bub« stacks up exactly like English ‘thumb-sucker boy.’

sentences.t:
Weh! jetzt geht es klipp und klapp
mit der Scher die Daumen ab,
mit der großen, scharfen Scher!
sentences.l:
Woe! now goes it snip and snap
with the shears the thumbs off,
with the big, sharp shears!
sentences.i:
Oh no! now — snip, snap —
off come his thumbs with the scissors,
with the big sharp scissors!
sentences.n: »es geht … ab« is impersonal: ‘it goes off’ ≈ ‘off they come’ — the »es« names no doer; the deed simply happens. »klipp und klapp« is pure sound-painting for the scissor blades. And the last line repeats »Scher« with mounting horror: not just any scissors — the big, sharp ones.

sentences.t: Hei! da schreit der Konrad sehr.
sentences.l: Hey! there screams the Konrad sorely.
sentences.n: »der Konrad« — spoken German often puts the article before a first name (‘the Konrad’). »schreit sehr« sounds odd today, because »sehr« now only strengthens adjectives — but »sehr« originally meant ‘sorely, painfully,’ and here it still carries that old force. »Hei!« is a gleeful little cry: the poem’s mock cheer at the disaster.

sentences.t:
Als die Mutter kommt nach Haus,
sieht der Konrad traurig aus.
sentences.l:
When the mother comes to home,
looks the Konrad sad out.
sentences.i:
When his mother comes back home,
Konrad is a sorry sight.
sentences.n: »aussehen« (‘to look, appear’ — literally ‘to out-look’) is separable: »sieht … aus«, with the »aus« waiting at the very end. The same pattern as »geht … auf« and »schneidet … ab« — the little word at the end always belongs to the verb. Note also the present tense (»kommt«, »sieht«): the ending is told as if happening before your eyes.

sentences.t:
Ohne Daumen steht er dort,
die sind alle beide fort.
sentences.l:
Without thumbs stands he there,
those are all both gone.
sentences.i:
There he stands without his thumbs —
both of them gone for good.
sentences.n: »die« opening the second line is not ‘the’ but a demonstrative pronoun: ‘those (the thumbs).’ German loves starting a sentence this way — »die sind fort« = ‘they’re gone.’ »alle beide« = ‘both of them,’ emphatic — every last one of the two. No moral is drawn; the picture is the moral.

glossary.ab: off (separable prefix — here the ab- of abschneiden, ‘to cut off’)
glossary.alle: all — alle beide: both of them (emphatic)
glossary.allem: vor allem — above all (dative of alles)
glossary.als: when; as — als ob: as if
glossary.am: = an dem, on the — am Daumen lutschen: to suck one’s thumb
glossary.auf: open (separable prefix: geht … auf, ‘opens’, from aufgehen)
glossary.aus: out — ich geh aus: I’m going out; also the aus- of aussehen (sieht … aus: looks, appears)
glossary.bauz: bang! crash! (the sound of the door bursting open)
glossary.beide: both — alle beide: both of them
glossary.da: here, there — du bleibst da: you stay here; da geht …: there goes …
glossary.daher: along, this way — kommt daher: comes along (toward the speaker)
glossary.daumen: der Daumen — thumb (plural also die Daumen: thumbs)
glossary.daumen-lutscher-bub: the thumb-sucker boy (Daumen thumb + Lutscher sucker + Bub boy)
glossary.der: the (masculine); (colloquially before a name) der Konrad
glossary.die: the; (as pronoun) those, they — die sind fort: they are gone
glossary.frau: die Frau — lady; die Frau Mama: the lady mama (old nursery style)
glossary.geh: go (ich geh = ich gehe, the -e dropped for meter — from gehen)
glossary.geht: goes (gehen) — geht … auf: opens; es geht … ab: off it comes
glossary.haus: das Haus — house; nach Haus: home(ward)
glossary.hübsch: nicely, properly (adverbial before another adjective; otherwise ‘pretty’)
glossary.klapp: snap! (scissor sound — klipp und klapp: snip, snap)
glossary.komm: come (ich komm = ich komme — from kommen)
glossary.lauf: der Lauf — run; in schnellem Lauf: at a run
glossary.lutsche: suck! (du-imperative of lutschen; am Daumen lutschen: to suck one’s thumb)
glossary.mehr: more — nicht … mehr: no more, no longer
glossary.scher: die Scher = die Schere — scissors, shears (clipped for the rhyme)
glossary.sehr: very (much); here in its old sense ‘sorely’ — schreit sehr: screams sorely
glossary.sieht: sieht … aus — looks, appears (aussehen); sehen: to see
glossary.sprach: spoke, said (old storybook past of sprechen)
glossary.stub: die Stub = die Stube — parlor, room (clipped for the rhyme)
glossary.türe: die Türe — door (older by-form of die Tür)
glossary.wupp: whoosh! pop! (the sound of the thumb popping in)
glossary.wär: = wäre — were, would be (subjunctive of sein)
